metadata.summary: Git-based dotfiles with GNU stow for symlinks. Per-category packages, machine-specific overrides, bootstrap script. Strict rules on what must never be committed.

content.title: Dotfiles Management
content.summary: Git-based dotfiles management using GNU stow for symlink creation. Covers repository structure with per-category packages (bash, git, tmux, vim, ssh, scripts), machine-specific overrides (machine-server/machine-workstation), bootstrap scripts for new machines, and strict privacy rules about what must never be committed.

why.p: A reproducible developer environment means a fresh server is productive in minutes, not hours. Stow creates $HOME symlinks from the repo so configs are version-controlled yet live at their expected paths. Machine-specific overrides via separate stow packages keep server and workstation configs cleanly separated without branching.

list.item: Setting up a new server and need to deploy the developer's standard config.
Rebuilding a server after data loss — dotfiles bootstrap restores the dev environment.
Standardizing configuration across multiple machines (workstation + VPS).
Version-controlling shell/editor configs for reproducibility and change tracking.
Configs containing secrets (SSH private keys, .env files, .bash_history) — never in dotfiles.
Environments managed by Ansible/Puppet where dotfiles deployment conflicts with CM.
Shared servers where one developer's dotfiles pollute others' environments.
Disposable containers where the filesystem is ephemeral.

rule.statement: Organize dotfiles as per-category stow packages (bash/, git/, tmux/, vim/, ssh/) so individual packages can be installed on any machine independently.
rule.rationale: A VPS needs bash, git, tmux, and ssh config but not vim GUI plugins or workstation browser bookmarks. Package isolation lets bootstrap pick only what is relevant to the machine type.

rule.statement: Mirror the $HOME directory structure inside each stow package; stow creates symlinks at $HOME that point into the package directory.
rule.rationale: stow uses the directory tree under each package as a template for $HOME. A file at dotfiles/bash/.bash_aliases creates ~/.bash_aliases as a symlink. This is stow's core design — match the target directory tree.

example.code:
# Install stow sudo apt install -y stow
# From the dotfiles root, stow individual packages cd ~/dotfiles stow bash # Creates ~/.bashrc, ~/.bash_aliases, etc. stow git tmux vim # Multiple packages at once
# Dry run before stowing (see what will happen) stow -n -v bash
# Restow after editing a config (remove + recreate symlinks) stow -R tmux
# Unstow (remove symlinks for a package) stow -D vim
# Handle an existing file: backup first, then stow mv ~/.bashrc ~/.bashrc.backup stow bash

antipattern.description: Using stow --adopt to absorb existing files into the package without reviewing the result.
antipattern.reason: --adopt moves the existing file into the stow package, potentially overwriting your dotfiles repo version. Always review git diff after --adopt to confirm the absorbed content is what you want to keep.

rule.statement: Keep machine-specific config in separate stow packages (machine-server/, machine-workstation/) rather than branching the git repo per machine.
rule.rationale: Branches require merging and diverge over time. Separate stow packages on the same branch can coexist on any machine; only the relevant one is stowed at bootstrap time. The repo stays in sync across all machines.

example.code:
# machine-server/.bashrc.d/server.sh — loaded only on servers export EDITOR=vim alias services='systemctl --user list-units --type=service' alias health='bash /srv/nero/health-check.sh'
# machine-workstation/.bashrc.d/workstation.sh — loaded only on workstations export EDITOR=code alias open='xdg-open'
# bootstrap.sh: detect machine type and stow the right package MACHINE_TYPE="server" # or detect from hostname, GUI presence, etc. stow "machine-$MACHINE_TYPE"

section.name: modular-bashrc

rule.statement: Use a .bashrc.d/ directory pattern sourced by .bashrc so machine-specific files, tool integrations, and aliases can be added/removed without editing the main .bashrc.
rule.rationale: A monolithic .bashrc becomes hard to maintain across machines. .bashrc.d/ enables modular addition: each stow package can add its own .bashrc.d/*.sh file without conflicting with others.

example.code: # ~/.bashrc — sources all .bashrc.d/ files if [ -d "$HOME/.bashrc.d" ]; then for f in "$HOME/.bashrc.d"/*.sh; do [ -r "$f" ] && source "$f" done unset f fi

section.reference: Full bootstrap script: clone dotfiles, install stow, backup conflicts, stow packages, detect machine type

section.name: dotfiles-privacy

rule.statement: Never commit SSH private keys (id_rsa, id_ed25519, id_ecdsa) even if they have a passphrase; commit only .ssh/config.
rule.rationale: A passphrase-protected private key can be brute-forced offline once exfiltrated from a public or semi-public git repository. The SSH config file (host aliases, identity file paths) contains no secrets.

rule.statement: Never commit .bash_history or .zsh_history; they may contain passwords typed directly on the command line, API tokens from curl commands, or secret paths.
rule.rationale: Shell history is a rich source of inadvertent secret leakage: API keys passed as -H flags, passwords typed instead of using a variable, SSH commands with usernames. History files belong in .gitignore.

rule.statement: Never commit Claude Code project configs (.claude/projects/); they may contain project-specific instructions referencing internal paths, API endpoints, or credential patterns.
rule.rationale: Claude project configs are machine- and project-specific. They accumulate references to local file paths, internal service URLs, and sometimes credentials captured during setup. They should live locally, not in a shared repo.

list.item: SAFE: .ssh/config (host aliases, identity file paths, ServerAlive settings)
SAFE: .gitconfig (user name, email, aliases, editor preference)

section.reference: .gitignore for the dotfiles repo covering all sensitive paths

antipattern.description: Using a public GitHub repository for dotfiles without a comprehensive .gitignore.
antipattern.reason: Public dotfiles repos are indexed by search engines and scraped by secret-scanning tools. A single committed private key or API token is a permanent breach even if deleted later (git history).

template.purpose: Clone repo, install stow, backup conflicts, stow all packages, detect machine type
.gitignore for the dotfiles repo: SSH keys, history, secrets, editor state
